module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So today we are continuing with Weber and one of his most famous writings, the Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism, which was published as 2 pieces in 1904 and 1905. So let's just keep the year in mind because Marx published his work approximately 100 years ago. So we had Adam Smith, who was at the beginning of the Industrial Revolution, and who was very excited about the potential of division of labor, the industrial revolution in making all of society prosperous. Then you had Marx who came in about 100 years after when they have actually seen some of the pros, but also the cons of what Industrial Revolution actually has meant in practice for many workers. And now we see Weber who is coming another 100 years, approximately 100 years after Marx. Weber is less concerned about whether capitalism is a good system or not, or whether it should be replaced with a different system. Weber is taking as given that capitalism is here to stay. And he is now asking two important questions, which is one, where did this capitalism come from? How did the feudalist monarch type of society, how did that change into this capitalistic rationalistic society? So first question, where did this capitalism even come from? And second, where is it going? So Weber is not unlike Marx. Weber is not really interested in saying whether capitalism is a good system or a bad system, and should it be replaced. He's taking capitalism as given and asking where did it come from and where is it going? So that's sort of the historical context. We are now 100 years after Marx. So who was Max Weber? He was a German sociologist, historian, political economist. He is considered one of the founders of modern sociology, and he's best known for his work on rationalization bureaucratization and the foundation of these ideas are created in this book, 'The Protestant Ethic and the spirit of Capitalism', which is what we will be discussing today. So the major focus of his life work was this process of rationalization, secularization, and the disenchantment in modern society, which he believed was linked to the rise of capitalism. And so, you know, this is, these are ideas which are still very relevant to us today because we are still having discussions about the increasing rationalization, increasing secularization, and increasing disenchantment in modern society. And the consequences of that in next module when we discuss Durkheim, we will look at more into the consequences of this kind of rationalization, secularization, disenchantment in modern society which many of these scholars believed was linked to the rise of capitalism. So why is Weber interesting to us as social theorists? Couple of reasons, one, his ideas are very much contrasting to Marx in some sense. So Marx talks about how the economics, the material conditions. The mode of production, the relations of production, the instruments of production, they are the foundation and then all of this other stuff, religion, ideology, belief system, morality, value system art. All of this are sort of the super structure, which is essentially meant to maintain whatever the base is. Marx is saying the exact opposite. He is talking about how ideology, and in his case he is specifically talking about religious ideology, but it can be for us as social theorists, it doesn't really matter any kind of ideology. It can be feminism, it can be communism, it can be, you know, any religion. Any kind of belief system, that's what interests us. How does a belief system held by some group of people, how does that shape economic life? In addition, as we discussed before, his views on the rationalization of modern life, secularization and disenchantment, those are also views that help us to understand some of the societal changes that we ourselves are looking at. And finally, from a more scholarly perspective Weber really advocated the use of interpretive methods, qualitative methods, to understand social change and social action. So these are the reasons why Weber is interesting to us as social theorists. Now let's look at a little bit of his life and education. He was born in Germany. Weber's family moved to Berlin when he was very young. His father was a high ranking bureaucrat while his mother was a devout Calvinist. And so he grew up in this household with very contrasting set of value systems and belief systems. So you have his dad, who is this man of the world, sophisticated, worldly, lavish lifestyle, and on the other hand, his mother is a devout Calvinist. Now Calvinism is one of the sets of Protestantism and they're very aesthetic. They believe in a very simple way of living. Any lavish way of living, they are suspicious of they think that is worldliness and going against spirituality. And so Weber kind of grew up with these two contrasting ideologies within his own household. And so is it any wonder that when he looked at the modern European world, this is where he was? So when he looked around him at his society, he also saw the tussle between these contrasting belief systems. So Weber starts with an analysis of what's really the spirit of capitalism. And when he says spirit, he means what is the ethics, the philosophy behind capital. So how do you go about, you know, just think for yourself if you had to, if someone told you your class assignment is that you have to identify the spirit of capitalism, how do you go about doing it? And so this, this is something that at the end we will propose to you as an exercise. But let's now look at how did Weber solve this problem? So he wanted to identify what is the spirit, the philosophy, the ethics of capitalism. And he decided to do this by looking at Ben Franklin's writings because Ben Franklin's writings were extremely popular and famous, and they really exemplified the spirit of capitalism and also Ben Franklin came from that in the United States where capitalism really flourished first. And so in some ways, Ben Franklin exemplifies the spirit of capitalism. And so Weber analyzes Ben Franklin's writings to get at this pure spirit of capitalism. So what is this capitalist mindset from Ben Franklin's writing? So Ben Franklin's writings are very much focusing on punctuality, honesty, frugality, continuous hard work, and he says how all of these help you to build a business to get credit and to be able to invest and grow further. So what are specifically, what are his maxims? One is time is money, and Ben Franklin gives an example. So, for instance, if I am earning 100 rupees a day, let's say if I decide that I actually need only 50 rupees to live in a okay life, you know, I, it's enough for me to live comfortably. And so I decide to sit idle for half a day because half a day's work will give me 50 rupees and I'll spend only 5 rupees. So actually I am still better off because I worked half a day and I haven't really spent everything. I've spent only 5 rupees, so I'm living a comfortable life. But according to Ben Franklin, this is a very wrong way of thinking about it because although you think that you have only spent 5 rupees, what you have actually spent is, or actually you have thrown away, according to Ben Franklin, the 50 rupees that you could have earned if you had worked the whole day. So actually what you have spent is 55 rupees. So it is not just what you have earned, but also what you'll lose if you don't work. So according to him is an example of this idea that time is money and there's an opportunity cost. Another maximum, Ben Franklin is, credit is money. Because money creates money. So Ben Franklin teaches that money is this very prolific generating nature. That's the nature of money. So 5 rupees will turn to 6, which it will turn to 7.3, which it will turn to even more. So this is the basic principle of compounding interest, right. So he goes on in a, in the same example of this person who has lost 55 rupees by working just half a day. Ben Franklin says, actually, it's not just the 55 rupees you've lost. But the remaining 50 rupees that you could have earned, if you had worked that 50 rupees, you could have invested earned interest. And if you keep accumulating this compounding, compounding year after year after year, by the time this young me becomes an old person. That decision to sit idle for half a day has actually cost me a considerable amount of money. So the 2 values that Ben Franklin is talking about is time, is money and credit is money because money creates money. So he focuses on this idea of industry, continuously working, frugality, and being very fair. And he says that if you want to build a business, unless you have all this, you cannot get credit, you cannot invest. You will not be a good business person. So he says the Good Pay master is lot of another man's purse. He that is known to pay punctually and exactly to the time he promises many at any time, and on any occasion can raise all the money that his friends can spare. So he's saying that if you always pay back on time, then when you actually need credit, you will never lack in credit. Everybody will be willing to give you credit. And so he says you should never keep borrowed money for even an hour more than you promised, because if you do that, then the next time your business really needs the money, then you will not be able to borrow. So this is very similar and recognizable for most of you. This is basically the idea of credit worthiness. Nowadays, we actually have, you know credit scores, credit worthiness scores, which actually quantify this same quality that Ben Franklin is talking about. Another point that he really focuses on is hard work. So he says that the most trifling actions that affect a man's credit are to be regarded. The sound of your hammer at 5 in the morning or 8 at night heard by a creditor makes him sleep easy 6 months longer, but if he sees you at a billiard table or hears your voice at a tavern when you should be at work, he immediately sends for his money and demands it back. Ben Franklin also talks about how you should not spend, you should invest. So he says, beware of thinking that all your own that you possess and of living accordingly. Instead, keep an exact amount of your expenses and your income because small trifling expenses will add up. And if you keep track of it, you will discover that if you had instead saved and invested those without causing yourself too much inconvenience, you will have a lot more in future because all of these add up. So what is the ethic here which Weber is identifying as the spirit of capitalism? So it, it's what is being preached here by Ben Franklin is not just a means of making money. It's an ethic. The infraction of its rules is treated not just as foolishness, but as a dereliction of duty. So if you don't do all this, instead of working one full day, you worked only half a day. It's not just that you're foolish and you have, you know, a lot of money that you could have got after, you know, 10 years, 20 years. So it's not just that you are foolish and you've lost all that, but you are morally deficient. You have derelicted your duty. So it is not just an expression of are you good at commercial business activities or it is not just some kind of personality inclination, so it is not morally neutral. It is ethically colored. It's a maxim on how you should live and think and be. So this is what Weber identifies as the ethics that underlie capitalism. It's a philosophy that provides a moral basis for moneymaking. So moneymaking is not greed, but it becomes a moral duty from being something negative, suddenly it has transformed into something which is your moral duty and a sign of your moral behavior. And this is the spirit according to Weber, the ethos of modern capitalism. Now, Weber, it points out to us that actually, when you think about this is not a natural way of thinking. So how did Ben Franklin even come to this idea that this is how we should live? One is he was reflecting the value systems of the place that he was in. He was in this New England, Massachusetts, that area of the United States, and he was expressing those value systems. But Ben Franklin also says that he realized the utility of frugality honesty through divine revelation. So Weber says this earning of more and more of money and the strict avoidance of all forms of personal enjoyment and pleasure. So the earning of money itself is a duty. So from this point of view, if you really think about it, it's kind of irrational. You want to earn money so that you can be happy. But here what is being said is that you have to keep away from all this personal enjoyment, pleasure, all of that, and yet you have to work as if this is your calling, your moral duty. And so it's actually kind of an irrational idea. So this idea of one's beauty as a calling, this according to Weber, is the most characteristic of the social ethic of capitalism. It's an obligation which the individual is supposed to feel or does feel towards her or his professional activity no matter what the activity is. Now, according to Weber, this ideology and belief system existed even before capitalism. Capitalism could not have developed without having had this ideology, and Weber tries to identify where did this somewhat irrational ideology, where did it even come from? Although Weber acknowledges that today, it appears to us as a very taken for granted. We all think so. We think our work, we should have passion for it. We should have motivation for it. We take it for granted. This idea of work as a calling and we don't even question it. But to Weber, he wants to identify, well, it wasn't always like that. The traditional idea was not that money making is a good thing. In fact, the traditional idea was that money making is somewhat, you know, morally questionable activity, it's greedy. So Weber is asking, how did this idea even come about? And this we will discuss in the next clip.
